focus_areas: Morsel-driven parallelism: partition 59M lineitem rows into ~4K-5K morsels of 10-15K rows each for 64 cores; each thread executes vectorized date filter and local hash aggregation (6 groups only)
Per-morsel partial aggregation: each morsel produces 6 partial sums/counts, final thread merges 6*64=384 partial results (negligible overhead)
SIMD vectorization on l_shipdate filter using AVX2 (process 4-8 date comparisons per instruction)
Compact hash table for 6-group aggregation per morsel (low cardinality = fast lookups, minimal allocation)
Column pruning: load only hot columns (l_shipdate, l_returnflag, l_linestatus, l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_tax) from columnar storage
strategy_notes: Q1 is embarrassingly parallel with minimal filter selectivity (94%) but low-cardinality aggregation (6 groups). Focus on full core utilization via morsel queues: each of 64 threads pulls independent morsels, executes vectorized filter, aggregates locally, deposits partial result. Avoid cross-thread synchronization in hot loop. Dictionary encoding on l_returnflag/l_linestatus enables integer aggregation keys without string overhead. Compile query-specific tight loop (scan → filter → aggregate) with template specialization for column types. Expected: near-linear speedup with 64 cores.
reasoning: TPC-H SF10 is a parallel analytical workload dominated by large table scans (59M-60M rows) with low-cardinality aggregations. GenDB has 64 cores available but current performance is competitive only on Q1 (48ms vs 86ms DuckDB). Q3 (386ms) and Q6 (29.3ms) indicate suboptimal parallelism, join strategy, and filter vectorization. Primary optimization levers: (1) morsel-driven thread parallelism across all three queries, (2) SIMD vectorization for complex predicates (Q6 especially), (3) early filter pushdown in Q3 three-way join, (4) per-morsel partial aggregation to minimize synchronization overhead.
